title: A Centenary of Justice — Front Matter (Foreword, Preface, Centennial Prayer)
sub_topics: Act 136 of 1901 (Judiciary Law) — June 11, 1901 SC founding
Davide Foreword inhibition acknowledgment in Estrada decision
100 ponencias + 10 separate opinions in his 6th year
650 ponencias + 60 separate opinions in 6 years total
Justice Reyes retirement and Carpio replacement
Old Testament Ten Commandments vs New Testament two commandments

stances.claim: Act 136 of 1901 (the Judiciary Law) established for the first time an *independent* judicial hierarchy with the Supreme Court at its apex — prior equivalents (Royal Audiencia, American military regime SC) were 'hardly more than extensions of the executive department.' The Court therefore reckons June 11, 1901 as its founding.

Decision-making is his primary duty; the centenary celebrations only his secondary task — yet he 'worked double time, even triple time' to produce 100+ ponencias and 10 separate opinions while chairing the centenary ExeCom.

He uses simple words and simple sentences deliberately — 'I want to be understood not only by lawyers, but also by their clients who read my work… that even high school graduates would comprehend me.'

In the Old Testament, God laid down only Ten Commandments; in the New Testament, Christ reduced them to two (love God / love neighbor) — yet 'throughout history, humans have complicated these simple commands by promulgating countless constitutions, codes, statutes and rules,' so 'it now takes almost a lifetime of patient study to know that we really do not know the law.'
stances.rhetorical_move: theological-genealogy of legal complexity + aphoristic paradox

stances.claim: Court collegiality is amazing — 'after heated discussions and debates, fifteen learned men and women… could still keep their collegial relationship genuinely friendly and cordial. Indeed, we daily agree to disagree without being disagreeable and to differ without being difficult.'
stances.rhetorical_move: institutional-collegiality framing + chiasmic aphorism (recurring CJ phrase)

stances.claim: Human law is awesome in its complexity — 'the challenge is always how to express it in all simplicity, so that it can be appreciated and obeyed in all sincerity.'
stances.rhetorical_move: aphoristic closer on the law-as-craft principle

institutions: 3rd Municipal Circuit Court of Sarrat-Vintar Ilocos Norte
cases: Estrada v. Desierto / Estrada v. Arroyo (referenced in Davide Foreword)
laws_treaties: New Testament Two Commandments (Matthew 22:34-40, Mark 12:28-31, John 13:34)

signature_phrases: agree to disagree without being disagreeable
in all simplicity, so that it can be appreciated and obeyed in all sincerity
we daily agree to disagree without being disagreeable and to differ without being difficult
it now takes almost a lifetime of patient study to know that we really do not know the law
register_markers: Tagalog code-switch (Katarungan at Bayan Magpakailanman, Abangan!, maraming salamat po)
decision_framework_signals: applies institutional-independence framing (Act 136's significance) as the operative criterion for SC's founding date
uses primary-vs-secondary-duty framing for managing competing institutional responsibilities
frames legal-writing accessibility as a deliberate ethic (write so high-school graduates can comprehend)
uses Old-Testament-vs-New-Testament theological-genealogy to comment on legal-complexity proliferation
uses collegiality-as-discipline framing for institutional cohesion despite ideological diversity
cross_references: page 6 of my last book, Transparency, Unanimity & Diversity
Appendix D of this book (tribute to Justice Reyes)
Isagani A. Cruz, 'Grammar lessons again,' Philippine Daily Inquirer, September 9, 2001
notable_anecdotes: Davide and Panganiban voluntarily inhibiting from the unanimous Estrada-Arroyo presidential-succession decision — 'our recusal has further served to strengthen the independence of the judiciary and the rule of law' (Davide Foreword)
Court replacing its old oval conference table with a new U-shaped one
Reducing the case backlog from ~6,000 (1986) → ~2,000 (1998) → ~1,000 (October 2000) → less than 50 (October 2001) via the centenary 'zero backlog' program
Chief judicial staff officer Francisco Quilala leaving for an Ilocos Norte judgeship; Emma Matammu and Licel Lopez departing for LLM studies at Illinois and Cambridge respectively
